questions.questionText: Which test is primarily used to differentiate Staphylococcus species from Streptococcus species?
questions.explanation: Staphylococci are catalase-positive; Streptococci are catalase-negative.

questions.questionText: Staphylococcus aureus is uniquely identified among Staphylococci by a positive:
questions.explanation: S. aureus produces coagulase, converting fibrinogen to fibrin.

questions.questionText: Protein A is an important virulence factor of S. aureus because it:
questions.optionA: Binds the Fc portion of IgG, preventing opsonization and phagocytosis
questions.explanation: By binding IgG backward, it hides from phagocytic cells.

questions.questionText: Rheumatic fever is a post-infectious, non-suppurative complication of infection with:
questions.explanation: Follows Group A streptococcal pharyngitis due to molecular mimicry.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia and adult bacterial meningitis is:
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is a lancet-shaped, gram-positive diplococcus.

questions.questionText: Which organism is the most common cause of subacute infective endocarditis following dental procedures?
questions.explanation: Viridans strep are normal oral flora that can seed diseased heart valves.

questions.questionText: Streptococcus agalactiae (Group B Strep) is most notable for causing:
questions.explanation: GBS is a major cause of morbidity in newborns passing through a colonized birth canal.

questions.questionText: Which mechanism confers resistance to methicillin in MRSA?
questions.optionC: Alteration of penicillin-binding protein (PBP-2a) encoded by the mecA gene
questions.explanation: The altered target (PBP-2a) has low affinity for all beta-lactam antibiotics.
Enterocci have natural resistance to all cephalosporins.

questions.questionText: A young woman presents with a sudden onset of high fever, rash, desquamation, and hypotension. Which toxin is likely responsible?
questions.optionC: Toxic Shock Syndrome Toxin-1 (TSST-1) from S. aureus
questions.explanation: TSST-1 functions as a superantigen, causing a massive cytokine storm.

questions.questionText: In Gram Positive Cocci, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Microbiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Microbiology references for Gram Positive Cocci. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Gram Positive Cocci.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Gram Positive Cocci scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Gram Positive Cocci.
